## 6. Regression detection (recent language-related work)

Recent changes that affect perceived consistency:

| Change | File(s) | Effect |
|--------|---------|--------|
| Taxonomy-based categories | `exploreCategoryTaxonomy.ts`, `categoryLabels.ts`, `referenceStore.ts` | ✅ Improved AR labels; fallback always shows 4 categories |
| Taxonomy governorates | `governorateLabels.ts`, `referenceStore.ts` | ✅ Fixed AR picker; `nameEn` from API |
| `catalogFromApi` requires **both** gov + categories API | `referenceStore.ts`, `explore.tsx` | ⚠️ Warning shows if either endpoint fails |
| Removed `GovernoratePickerSheet` from Explore | `ExploreHeader.tsx` | ✅ Single source from store |
| Guest Explore still uses `MOCK_EVENTS` | `explore.tsx` | ❌ English content in AR mode |
| Search live API | `useEventSearch.ts`, `search.tsx` | ❌ Raw error messages in UI |

**Most likely “regression” users notice:** not broken dictionary keys, but **stricter catalog warning logic**, **English API/mock content**, and **RTL not reloading**.

---

## Affected files (by severity)

### Critical
- `mobile/src/utils/rtl.ts`
- `mobile/src/components/layout/RtlLayout.tsx`
- `mobile/app/(tabs)/explore.tsx`
- `mobile/src/mock/events.ts` (+ other mock PDP sources)
- `mobile/src/services/api/eventMap.ts`

### High
- `mobile/src/hooks/useEventSearch.ts`
- `mobile/app/search.tsx`
- All files listed in RC-3 (API error pattern)
- `mobile/src/store/referenceStore.ts`
- `mobile/src/utils/searchTrending.ts`

### Medium
- `mobile/app/(tabs)/_layout.tsx`
- `mobile/src/components/navigation/BusinessTabBar.tsx`
- `mobile/src/store/localeStore.ts` (`regionLabel`)
- `mobile/src/utils/bookingApiMap.ts`
- `mobile/src/services/api/userMap.ts`
- `mobile/src/bootstrap/hydrateAuthSession.ts`
- `mobile/src/i18n/dictionary.ts` (`explore.gov.*` unused)

### Low
- `mobile/src/components/sheet/PremiumSheetPickCard.tsx`
- `mobile/app/edit-profile.tsx`
- `mobile/app/(auth)/signup.tsx`
- `mobile/src/utils/apiError.ts`
- `mobile/src/api/http.ts`

### Admin Web (separate, generally healthy)
- Uses `useAdminI18n` + `document.dir` correctly
- API `governorateName` / category names in tables remain English (display uses `localizedGovernorateName` where wired)
- Same API-content limitation as mobile for dynamic rows

---

## Recommended fixes

### Quick wins (1–2 days)
1. **Prompt reload on locale change** — after `setLocale()`, show “Restart to apply layout” or call `expo-updates` reload / `RNRestart` in dev.
2. **Stop showing raw API errors** — map status codes to `t('errors.*')`; never pass `ApiError.message` to UI.
3. **Fix search error display** — use `t('search.loadError')` only; don’t pass raw `error` as message.
4. **Localize `referenceStore.errorMessage`** — use dictionary key or derive at render time from `locale`.
5. **Fix `suggestedTagsFromEvents`** — map subcategory slugs through taxonomy or hide tags when no localized label.
6. **Add `key={locale}` to `<Tabs>`** in `(tabs)/_layout.tsx` and business dashboard layout to force tab label refresh.
7. **Remove hardcoded `textAlign: 'left'`** — use `textAlignStart(isRTL)`.

### Medium refactors (3–5 days)
1. **Central error i18n helper** — `mapApiError(e, t)` used everywhere.
2. **Unify reference labels** — single module for gov + category labels; trim duplicate `explore.gov.*` keys.
3. **Guest Explore** — sign-in empty state instead of English mocks in AR mode (or bilingual mock content).
4. **Sync locale with user profile** on login if backend adds `preferredLanguage`.
5. **Remove `regionLabel`** from localeStore; use `selectedCityId` + governorate catalog everywhere.

### Larger refactors
1. **Backend bilingual fields** — `nameEn`/`nameAr` for events, categories, governorates.
2. **Content locale strategy** — separate “UI locale” vs “content locale” with clear UX when content is untranslated.
3. **RTL-first navigation** — revisit forced LTR tab bars vs mirrored tab order in Arabic.
4. **Automated hardcoded-string scan** — extend `check:i18n` with ESLint rule or script for literal strings in TSX.

---

## Summary table

| Issue | Root cause | User-visible symptom | Severity |
|-------|------------|----------------------|----------|
| Layout doesn’t flip fully | No reload after `forceRTL` | Chevrons/text ok, inputs/tabs odd | Critical |
| Mixed EN/AR on Explore | API + mock content English | Arabic UI, English events | Critical |
| Errors stay English | Raw `ApiError.message` | Arabic screen, English error | High |
| Tab labels stuck | Navigator option caching | Old tab titles | Medium |
| Search tags English | Tags from API venue names | Arabic search, English chips | Medium |
| Catalog warning | Partial API failure flag | Warning despite usable fallback | Medium |
| Profile language | No server sync | Account switch doesn’t change language | Medium |

## Completed

### 1. Locale change reliability
- Language switch calls `reloadAppForLocaleChange()` — syncs native RTL, persists locale, then full app reload
- Added `key={locale}` on main tabs and Business Hub tabs so labels/navigation remount after locale change

--- 1332. ASSISTANT ---
Fixed. Here’s what went wrong and what changed.

## Root cause

With `I18nManager.forceRTL`, React Native **mirrors** `textAlign: 'left'` / `'right'`. Our helper returned `'right'` for Arabic, which landed on the **visual left** — the opposite of what we wanted.

The extra `width: '100%'` and `alignSelf: 'stretch'` on labels then made that worse across the form.

## Fix

`textAlignStart()` now always returns `'left'` (RN mirrors it to the visual start/right in Arabic). `formAlignStyle()` is text-only again — no width or flex overrides.

## Files changed

| File | Why |
|------|-----|
| `src/utils/rtlText.ts` | Correct start/end alignment for mirrored RTL; strip layout overrides from `formAlignStyle` |
| `src/components/auth/AuthTextField.tsx` | Reverted global label/helper alignment; removed `width: '100%'` on wrap |
| `src/components/forms/BusinessPartnerCategorySelectField.tsx` | Kept `formAlignStyle` on label + value only; removed wrap width |
| `src/components/forms/GovernorateSelectField.tsx` | Same as category field |
| `src/components/business/BusinessFormField.tsx` | Reverted multiline label override; removed wrap width |
| `app/business/(dashboard)/profile.tsx` | Reverted section titles/helper overrides; restored natural RTL for most fields; kept category/governorate + phone fixes |

## Preserved

- Phone `+962` prefix and LTR digit editing (`phoneInputStyle`)
- Category/governorate label + value right alignment via corrected `formAlignStyle`
- English layout unchanged (mirroring only applies in Arabic RTL mode)
- Logo/cover camera badges

`npm run typecheck` passes.

## Layout fixes

### 1. Business identity (hero)
- Business name and tagline are always **`textAlign: 'center'`** in Arabic and English
- Hero block uses `alignItems: 'center'` so text stays centered under the logo
- Lead line centered for a balanced identity section

- Replaced oversized flag (`h2`) with a **20px emoji** aligned to the +962 code
- Flag, +962, divider, and digits share **`lineHeight: 22`** and `alignItems: 'center'`
- Prefix sits in shared `BusinessFieldLeadSlot` (vertically centered in the row)

### 3. Email & technical inputs

  - **`BusinessFieldIconSlot`** — 40×40 icon container
  - **`BusinessFieldLeadSlot`** — vertically centers leading content
  - **`businessFieldInputStyle`** — `textAlignVertical: 'center'`, zero vertical padding, Android `includeFontPadding: false`
- `AuthTextField` uses these for all technical rows (email, phone, URLs)

### 4. Consistency pass
All icon + text rows now share:
- **54px min row height**

--- 1351. ASSISTANT ---
TextInput is getting a fixed row height plus a smaller `lineHeight`, which pushes text toward the top on iOS. Switching to a flex-centered wrapper so the control stays on the icon’s center line.

[REDACTED]

[Tools used: StrReplace, StrReplace, StrReplace, StrReplace, StrReplace, StrReplace, StrReplace, StrReplace, StrReplace, StrReplace]

--- 1352. ASSISTANT ---
## Root cause

Text was applied directly on `TextInput` with **height 54px + lineHeight 22px + paddingTop**. On iOS that combination pushes glyphs toward the **top** of the field, while icons stay centered in their 40×40 slots — so text looked high relative to icons.

## Fix (alignment only — no color/spacing/logic changes)

### Shared row centering (`businessFieldRow.tsx`)

| Export | Role |
|---|---|
| `businessFieldInputSlotStyle()` | 54px flex slot with `justifyContent: 'center'` |
| `businessFieldInputControlStyle()` | TextInput: 22px line box, zero padding |
| `businessFieldValueSlotStyle()` | Picker value area: 54px, vertically centered |
| `businessFieldInlineClusterStyle` | Phone prefix cluster: fixed 22px height, `alignItems: 'center'` |

**Pattern:** row height centers the slot; the slot centers the 22px text control — same center line as icons.

### `AuthTextField.tsx`

- Wraps `TextInput` in a centering slot View
- Control uses `businessFieldInputControlStyle()` (no row-height padding on the input itself)
- Applies to **Email, Phone, Website, Maps URL**

### Pickers

- **Governorate** and **Category** use `businessFieldValueSlotStyle()` so display text aligns with input rows

### Phone prefix (`profile.tsx`)

- Flag, `+962`, separator, and digits share `businessFieldInlineClusterStyle` (22px height)
- Flag/code use matching `lineHeight` + `height` so they sit on the same line as the centered phone input

## Result

All icon-input rows now share:

- Fixed **54px** row height  
- `alignItems: 'center'`  
- Icon slot centered  
- Text slot/control centered on the **same vertical axis**

`npm run typecheck` passes.

--- 1357. ASSISTANT ---
## Summary

Vertical alignment is now handled through one shared system in `businessFieldRow.tsx`, with platform-specific centering where React Native needs it.

### Root cause
On **iOS**, `TextInput` does not center reliably with `height + paddingTop + small lineHeight`. The fix is **`lineHeight === height`** on the control. On **Android**, use `textAlignVertical: 'center'` + `includeFontPadding: false` inside a flex-centered slot.

### Shared system (`businessFieldRow.tsx`)

| Export | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `businessFieldInputControlStyle()` | Single-line inputs — iOS: 54px height/lineHeight; Android: 22px centered in slot |
| `businessFieldInputSlotStyle()` | Row text slot — Android uses `justifyContent: 'center'` |
| `businessFieldValueTextStyle()` / `BusinessFieldValueText` | Picker values use **identical** font metrics as inputs |
| `businessHeroTitleControlStyle()` | Business name — 40px centered line box |
| `businessHeroTaglineControlStyle()` | Tagline — 34px centered line box |
| `businessMultilineInputStyle()` | Description — top-aligned, zero padding, no clipping |
| `businessFieldInlineClusterStyle` | Phone prefix cluster — shared 22px center line |

### Field-by-field

| Field | Fix |
|---|---|
| **Business Name** | `businessHeroTitleControlStyle()` — no conflicting `inputTextStyle` overrides |
| **Tagline** | `businessHeroTaglineControlStyle()` — 16px/22px metrics, not body-lg 24px |
| **Category** | `BusinessFieldValueText` replaces `AppText` — matches input text exactly |
| **Description** | Consistent line metrics, `padding: 0`, top-aligned in existing box |
| **Email / Website / Maps URL** | iOS full-row lineHeight centering via `AuthTextField` |
| **Phone** | Flag in centered wrap; `+962` uses `businessFieldInputTextStyle`; digits use shared input control |
| **Governorate** | `BusinessFieldValueText` — aligned like a text input |

### Unchanged
Colors, spacing, RTL/LTR, icons, and business logic.

`npm run typecheck` passes.

--- 1360. ASSISTANT ---
Identifying the issue: iOS `TextInput` with `height: 54` and `lineHeight: 54` inside a nested slot causes text to render below the row. Simplifying the row structure and relying on `alignItems: 'center'` on the row instead.

[REDACTED]

[Tools used: Write, Write]

--- 1361. ASSISTANT ---
## Root cause

The previous refactor set **iOS `TextInput` to `height: 54` + `lineHeight: 54`** inside a nested slot that also had `height: 54`. On iOS that pushes the rendered text **below** the input bounds, so values appeared under the rounded row border (especially visible in RTL).

## Fix — row structure, not just metrics

### New shared row layout (`businessFieldRow.tsx`)

- **`businessFieldRowLayoutStyle`** — fixed 54px row, `alignItems: 'center'`, `overflow: 'hidden'`
- **`businessFieldInputFlexStyle()`** — `flex: 1` on the input (no wrapper slot, no fixed input height on iOS)
- **`businessFieldInputControlStyle()`** — typography only; Android gets `height: 22` + `textAlignVertical: 'center'`
- **`businessFieldRowStyle()`** — adds `overflow: 'hidden'` + `maxHeight: 54`

### `AuthTextField` — structural change

**Before:**
```
Row → Slot (height 54) → TextInput (height 54, lineHeight 54)  ❌
```

**After:**
```
Row (alignItems: 'center') → TextInput (flex: 1, lineHeight 22, no iOS height)  ✅
```

Vertical centering comes from the **row’s `alignItems: 'center'`**, not from inflating `lineHeight` to match row height.

### Other updates

- **Email, Phone, Maps URL, Website** — all use the same `AuthTextField` / `BusinessIconTextField` path
- **Phone** — removed redundant `textInputStyle` override (`technicalInput` already handles LTR)
- **Category / Governorate pickers** — use `businessFieldRowLayoutStyle` + `BusinessFieldValueText`

### Unchanged

Icons, colors, spacing, RTL/LTR (`technicalInput` + `direction: 'ltr'`), and business logic.

`npm run typecheck` passes. Text should now sit inside the 54px rounded row, centered with icons.

[REDACTED]

--- 1365. ASSISTANT ---
## Summary

Phone prefix placement was inconsistent because **Edit Profile** and **Signup** used `leftSlot` without `technicalInput`, so in Arabic (RTL) React Native mirrored the prefix to the **right**. **Business Profile** already used `technicalInput: true` with `direction: 'ltr'`, which kept the prefix on the left.

- **`JordanPhonePrefix`** — fixed layout: `[ 🇯🇴 +962 | ]`
- **`JordanPhoneField`** — wraps `AuthTextField` with:
  - `technicalInput={true}` → row uses `direction: 'ltr'` + `flexDirection: 'row'`
  - `technicalInputStyle()` → digits stay LTR
  - Built-in prefix as `leftSlot` in `BusinessFieldLeadSlot`
- **`appearance`**: `'default'` (auth screens) | `'business'` (premium profile styling)

### Updated screens

| Screen | Change |
|---|---|
| **Edit Profile** | `JordanPhoneField` |
| **Signup** | `JordanPhoneField` |
| **Business Profile** | `JordanPhoneField` (`appearance="business"`) |
| **Business Join** | `JordanPhoneField` (was plain field, no prefix) |

### RTL hardening (`rtlText.ts`)

```ts
technicalFieldRowStyle = { direction: 'ltr', flexDirection: 'row' }
```

This prevents row mirroring so the prefix cluster always stays on the **physical left** in Arabic and English.

### Result

`[ 🇯🇴 +962 | Phone Number ]` — same layout in RTL and LTR; only digit text alignment follows locale rules via `technicalInputStyle`.

--- 1453. USER ---
Perform a complete Light Mode and Dark Mode visual audit of the entire admin-web application.

Goals:
1. Ensure every screen, component, modal, table, form, dropdown, badge, chart, icon, emoji, illustration, empty state, loader, toast, tooltip, sidebar, navbar, card, button, input, pagination, and settings page follows the design system.
2. Verify there are NO hardcoded colors that break the theme.
3. Verify all colors come from the theme tokens only.
4. Verify Light Mode and Dark Mode are visually consistent and feel like the same product.

Light Mode Requirements:
- Use the approved light palette only.
- Maintain strong readability and contrast.
- Text must always be easy to read.
- Icons and emojis must be clearly visible.
- Cards, tables, and forms must have proper separation from backgrounds.
- No washed-out sections.
- No white text on light backgrounds.
- No neon green backgrounds covering large areas.
- Accent colors should be used intentionally, not everywhere.

Dark Mode Requirements:
- Keep existing dark palette.
- Ensure all text, icons, emojis, badges, charts, and controls are readable.
- No low-contrast gray-on-gray combinations.
- No components that accidentally use light-mode colors.
- Verify hover, focus, active, disabled, and selected states.

Audit Checklist:
- Sidebar
- Header/Navbar
- Dashboard
- Business Profiles
- Users
- Events
- Bookings
- Ratings
- Reports
- Categories
- Governorates
- Settings
- Login Page

- Drawers
- Forms
- Tables
- Search Bars

- Empty States
- Loading States
- Error States
- Toasts
- Charts
- Statistics Cards
- Status Badges
- Pagination
- All Buttons
- All Icons
- All Emojis
- All Images/Illustrations

Required Fixes:
- Replace any hardcoded color with theme tokens.
- Fix any contrast issues.
- Fix any inconsistent spacing caused by theme changes.
- Fix hover/active/focus states.
- Fix icons that disappear or become difficult to see.
- Fix emoji visibility in both themes.
- Ensure accessibility-friendly contrast throughout the application.

Important:
- Do NOT redesign layouts.
- Do NOT modify business logic.
- Do NOT modify routes.
- Do NOT modify APIs.
- Do NOT modify data models.
- Only improve theme consistency, color usage, readability, and visual quality.

--- 1458. ASSISTANT ---
## Admin-web theme audit — complete

Full Light/Dark theme audit is done. Hardcoded legacy terracotta/plum colors were replaced with semantic `--vb-*` tokens, contrast issues were addressed, and the build passes.

---

### 1. Issues found

| Area | Issue | Status |
|------|--------|--------|
| **Sidebar (light)** | Green gradient wash, low-contrast nav | Fixed — pale mint `#EEF8E2`, dark text, lime active indicator |
| **Login page** | Terracotta/plum gradients, rgba shadows/borders | Fixed — `--vb-login-*` tokens |
| **Dashboard hero** | Legacy gradient overlay, plum-deep text | Fixed — `--vb-hero-*` tokens |
| **Welcome banner** | Terracotta/plum gradient | Fixed — `--vb-welcome-*` tokens |
| **Filter chips** | Hardcoded terracotta active bg | Fixed — `--vb-chip-active-bg` |
| **Stat cards / accents** | Terracotta gradient lines | Fixed — `--vb-gradient-primary` |
| **Rank list hover** | `rgba(196, 137, 108, 0.06)` | Fixed — `--vb-hover-row` |
| **Table rows** | Selected/hover legacy rgba | Fixed — `--vb-hover-row-selected` / `--vb-hover-row` |
| **Empty states** | SVG fills hardcoded in TSX | Fixed — CSS via `--vb-empty-illustr-*` |
| **Business profile rejection** | Inline warning rgba styles | Fixed — `.vb-callout--warning` |
| **Shadow tokens** | Global `:root` shadows overrode light theme | Fixed — shadows scoped to theme blocks |
| **Topbar, badges, toasts, modals** | Mixed hardcoded colors | Fixed in prior pass — semantic tokens |
| **Dark mode sidebar active** | `rgba(196, 137, 108, 0.28)` | **Preserved** — existing dark palette (frozen) |
| **Charts** | Use `chartColors.terracotta` alias | OK — maps to `--vb-primary` per theme |
| **Page inline styles** | Layout-only (margin, flex, font-size) | OK — no color values |

No remaining hardcoded colors in component CSS or TSX outside theme definition files.

---

### 2. Files changed

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `admin-web/src/theme/palette-colors.css` | Approved light palette swatches |
| `admin-web/src/theme/colors.ts` | `lightPalette` updated |
| `admin-web/src/theme/admin-theme.css` | Light semantic tokens, sidebar/hero/login/callout/empty/shadow tokens; dark tokens preserved |

| `admin-web/src/components/ui/EmptyState.tsx` | Theme-aware SVG via CSS variables |
| `admin-web/src/pages/BusinessProfileDetailPage.tsx` | Rejection callout uses `.vb-callout--warning` |

--- 1481. ASSISTANT ---
The Admin Web color system is now **locked** as the approved baseline.

A persistent Cursor rule was added at `.cursor/rules/admin-web-theme-locked.mdc`. It enforces:

- **No color changes** (light/dark palettes, tokens, sidebar, header, page, cards, buttons, accents, shadows, borders) without your explicit approval
- **Future UI work** must reuse existing `--vb-*` / `--palette-*` tokens only — no hardcoded colors in components

--- 1514. ASSISTANT ---
## Root cause

Theme tokens were updated, but several components still bypassed them:

1. **Hardcoded neutral grays** — Business dashboard hero used `#111111` and `rgba(17, 17, 17, 0.75)` instead of `textPrimary` / `textSecondary`.
2. **Hardcoded slate/black overlays** — Event card favorite chip and photo remove button used cold gray/black rgba values.
3. **Icons using `colors.text`** — Header, sheet, and nav icons used primary text (`#1F2A1A`) or black rgba, which reads as gray on soft backgrounds.
4. **Avatar placeholders** — Profile avatars used `surfaceMuted` (#EEF8E2) backgrounds, making olive placeholder icons look washed out and gray.

## Hardcoded gray usages found & fixed

| Location | Was | Now |
|----------|-----|-----|
| `business/(dashboard)/home.tsx` | `#111111`, `rgba(17, 17, 17, 0.75)` | `colors.textPrimary`, `colors.textSecondary` |
| `EventCard.tsx` | `rgba(17, 24, 39, 0.45)` | `colors.iconOverlay` (new token) |
| `EventPhotosField.tsx` | `rgba(0,0,0,0.55)`, `rgba(255,250,245,0.92)` | `colors.icon`, `colors.card` |
| Nav/header icons (6 files) | `colors.text` | `colors.icon` |
| Avatar placeholders (me, edit-profile, business profile) | `surfaceMuted` / `primaryMuted` bg | `colors.card` + `colors.placeholder` icon |
| `booking.tsx` past status | `colors.textMuted` | `colors.disabled` |

**Left intentionally:** black rgba for photo overlays (`profile.tsx` cover fade), shadow `#000`, and dark-mode-only tokens in theme files.

## Files changed

- `mobile/src/theme/palettes.ts` — added `iconOverlay` light token
- `mobile/app/business/(dashboard)/home.tsx`
- `mobile/app/business/(dashboard)/profile.tsx`
- `mobile/src/components/cards/EventCard.tsx`
- `mobile/src/components/business/EventPhotosField.tsx`
- `mobile/app/(tabs)/me.tsx`
- `mobile/app/edit-profile.tsx`
- `mobile/app/(tabs)/booking.tsx`
- `mobile/src/components/layout/AppHeader.tsx`
- `mobile/src/components/explore/ExploreHeader.tsx`
- `mobile/app/search.tsx`
- `mobile/src/components/layout/DetailHeader.tsx`
- `mobile/src/components/sheet/PremiumSheetLayout.tsx`
- `mobile/src/components/auth/AuthScreenLayout.tsx`

--- 1530. ASSISTANT ---
Fixed yellow/neon text on light surfaces across the app. Summary:

## Root cause

`color="accent"` resolves to `#F0FB45` (neon yellow). It was used for **Popular now** chips, prices, links, and labels on **white/light** backgrounds — unreadable and off-brand.

## Fixes

**CategoryChip** (filters + catalog chips):
- Unselected: `#55644A` (`rowDescription`) on white cards
- Selected (light): `#1F2A1A` text, `#8BC249` border/icon accent, white fill
- Dark mode chip behavior unchanged

**Search screen**:
- Popular now tags → `rowDescription` (`#55644A`)
- Filters hint → `rowDescription`
- Result prices → `primaryLight` (`#8BC249`)
- Tag chips use white `card` background

**App-wide audit** — replaced `color="accent"` text with:
- **Prices / links / actions** → `primaryLight` (`#8BC249`)
- **Chip-style labels** → `rowDescription` (`#55644A`)
- **Section overlines** → `primaryLight` (not yellow body text)
- **Icons on light surfaces** → `primaryLight` instead of `colors.accent`

`accentText` (`#1F2A1A`) kept where it was already correct (filters Done, explore eyebrows).

--- 1577. ASSISTANT ---

--- 1578. ASSISTANT ---
# Startup flow fix

## Root cause

Cold start routing lived in `app/index.tsx` (splash). After store hydration it read persisted `hasCompletedOnboarding` from `appStore`:

- **`false`** → `router.replace('/entry')` (welcome)
- **`true`** → after 2.2s splash → `router.replace('/(tabs)/explore')`

`hasCompletedOnboarding` is set when the user taps **Browse First** on entry, uses **Browse first** on signup, or signs in. It stays in AsyncStorage, so the next launch skipped entry and went straight to **Explore**.

Other areas were already correct:

- **Logout** (`me.tsx`) → `logout()` clears the flag and `router.replace('/entry')`
- **Browse First** (`entry.tsx`) → explicit navigation to tabs
- **Login / signup** → explicit navigation to tabs after auth
- No persisted navigation state or auth guard forcing tabs on launch
- `(tabs)/_layout.tsx` `initialRouteName="explore"` only applies **inside** the tab navigator after the user opens tabs

## Files modified

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `mobile/app/index.tsx` | Always route to `/entry` after splash; removed `hasCompletedOnboarding` branch to Explore |

## Redirect / navigation logic changed

**Before:**

```text
Splash (index) → if !hasCompletedOnboarding → /entry
               → if hasCompletedOnboarding  → /(tabs)/explore  ❌
```

**After:**

```text
Splash (index) → always → /entry  ✓
```

Unchanged (by design):

- `entry.tsx` **Browse First** → `/(tabs)/explore`
- `login.tsx` / `signup.tsx` success → `/(tabs)/explore`
- `me.tsx` logout → `/entry`
- Tab `initialRouteName="explore"` when user is already in tabs

`hasCompletedOnboarding` remains in the store for browse/login/signup/logout state; it is no longer used for cold-start routing.

## Confirmations

| Requirement | Status |
|-------------|--------|
| Fresh open shows Entry (Log In / Sign Up / Browse First) | Yes — splash then `/entry` |
| Explore is not the startup screen | Yes — removed auto-redirect from splash |
| Browse First → tabs | Unchanged |
| Login → existing auth flow | Unchanged |
| Logout → Entry | Unchanged |
| No screen redesign / theme / backend changes | Confirmed |
| `npm run typecheck` | Pass |

**How to verify:** Force-quit the app, reopen → brief brand splash → Entry carousel with **Log In / Sign Up** and **Browse First**. After a previous session with Browse First or login, you should still land on Entry, not Explore.

[REDACTED]

--- 1595. ASSISTANT ---
# Admin Web dark theme — aligned with mobile

The Admin Web dark theme now uses the same palette and semantic tokens as the mobile app (`paletteColors.ts` + `designSystem.ts` dark theme). Light mode is unchanged.

## What changed

### Centralized token system
| File | Role |
|------|------|
| `admin-web/src/theme/palette-colors.css` | Full `darkMix` swatches synced with mobile |
| `admin-web/src/theme/dark-semantics.css` | **New** — single dark semantic layer (OS dark + `[data-theme='dark']`) |
| `admin-web/src/theme/admin-theme.css` | Imports `dark-semantics.css`; removed ~300 lines of duplicate/outdated dark vars |
| `admin-web/src/theme/colors.ts` | `darkMix`, `darkSemantic`, chart palette — mirrors mobile for TS/chart use |

### Removed (dark mode)
- Warm cream/plum sidebar colors (`rgba(247, 239, 231)`, terracotta active states)
- Duplicate `@media` + `[data-theme='dark']` blocks in `admin-theme.css`
- Inputs/modals using canvas-deep (same as page) with no card contrast

### Mobile-aligned dark semantics
- **Canvas:** `#000` → `#0A0A0A` gradient (`#1A1A1A` → `#0A0A0A`)
- **Cards / fields / login:** `#404040` charcoal
- **Elevated:** `#484848` table headers, hero asides
- **Primary:** `#C7FE1D` → `#F2F862` gradient CTAs
- **Status:** `#4ADE80` success, `#FFF30B` warning, `#F87171` error (+ mobile `errorBg`)
- **Borders:** `rgba(254,254,254,0.06/0.08)` — no alternate gray scale
- **Sidebar / nav:** Charcoal → black gradient, lime active indicator, lime icon accent
- **Shadows / glow:** Mobile dark shadow opacity + lime glow tokens

--- 1599. ASSISTANT ---
# Root cause

Dark tokens were defined correctly, but **they were never winning in the cascade**:

1. **`:root` was bound to the light theme** — `admin-theme.css` used `:root, [data-theme='light'] { … }`, so every light semantic variable was always set on `<html>`, even when OS dark mode or dark tokens applied later.

2. **No `data-theme` on `<html>`** — Nothing set `data-theme="dark"`. Dark mode relied only on `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)`, which does not apply if the OS/browser is in **light** mode (common on dev machines).

3. **Vite/Lightning CSS** — The bundle showed `:root,[data-theme=light]{--lightningcss-light:initial;…}` on `:root` unconditionally, reinforcing the light theme on the document root.

# Fixes applied

### Theme activation
- **`themeStore.ts`** — Persists theme (`dark` | `light` | `system`), sets `document.documentElement.dataset.theme`. **Default: `dark`.**
- **`index.html`** — `data-theme="dark"` on `<html>` for instant dark paint before JS.
- **`main.tsx`** — Imports `themeStore` first so theme applies on load.
- **Settings → Appearance** — Dark / Light / System toggle.

### CSS structure (import order)
```
palette-colors.css → light-semantics.css → dark-semantics.css → admin-theme.css → admin-components.css
```

- **Removed** unconditional `:root` light semantics from `admin-theme.css`.
- **`light-semantics.css`** — Light vars only on `html[data-theme='light']` or `@media (prefers-color-scheme: light) { :root:not([data-theme='dark']) }`.
- **`dark-semantics.css`** — Uses `html[data-theme='dark']` (not a generic `[data-theme='dark']`).

### Dev debug (DEV only)
- **`ThemeDebugPanel`** — Bottom-right panel shows store mode, `html[data-theme]`, and resolved `--vb-page-bg-base`, `--vb-card`, `--vb-primary`. Stripped in production builds (`import.meta.env.DEV`).

# Verify in browser

1. Hard refresh — you should see **black/charcoal** UI and **lime** accents immediately.
2. Open **Settings → Appearance** — switch Dark / Light / System.
3. In dev, check the debug panel: `html[data-theme]: dark`, `--vb-page-bg-base: #0a0a0a`, `--vb-card: #404040`, `--vb-primary: #c7fe1d`.
4. DevTools → `<html data-theme="dark">` should be present by default.

# Checks
- `npm run check:i18n` — pass  
- `npm run lint` — pass  
- `npm run build` — pass  
- Bundle no longer contains `:root,[data-theme=light]` on bare `:root`

When you’ve confirmed dark mode looks right, say if you want the dev **ThemeDebugPanel** removed.

--- 1602. USER ---
Please implement a complete PayPal Sandbox payment integration for my Vibook project using the official PayPal Sandbox tools:

https://developer.paypal.com/tools/sandbox/

Goal:
Add a safe test payment flow using PayPal Sandbox only, not live PayPal.

Requirements:

1. Use PayPal Sandbox environment only

* Base API URL:
  https://api-m.sandbox.paypal.com
* Do not use live PayPal endpoints.
* Add environment variables:
  PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID
  PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET
  PAYPAL_MODE=sandbox

2. Add backend PayPal payment flow
   Create a clean PayPal service in the backend that supports:

* Generate PayPal access token
* Create PayPal order
* Capture PayPal order
* Handle success/failure responses
* Store payment result linked to a booking

3. Add backend endpoints
   Add endpoints like:

POST /api/v1/payments/paypal/create-order

POST /api/v1/payments/paypal/capture-order

The create-order endpoint should receive:

* bookingId
* amount
* currency
* eventId
* selected time slot / ticket info if needed

The capture-order endpoint should receive:

* paypalOrderId
* bookingId

4. Booking integration
   Connect PayPal payment with the existing booking system:

* When the user starts checkout, create a booking with status PENDING.
* When PayPal order is created, keep booking as PENDING.
* When PayPal capture succeeds, update booking status to CONFIRMED.
* When capture fails or user cancels, keep booking as PENDING or update it to FAILED/CANCELLED depending on the existing enum.
* Never confirm a booking before PayPal capture succeeds.

5. Security rules

* Never expose PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET to the frontend or mobile app.
* Do not store user card details.
* Backend must be responsible for creating and capturing PayPal orders.
* Validate that the booking belongs to the logged-in user before payment.
* Validate the amount from the backend, not from the frontend, to prevent price manipulation.

6. Frontend / mobile checkout
   Add a PayPal Sandbox payment button or checkout action in the checkout screen.

Flow:

* User selects event, time slot, quantity/ticket.
* App creates or retrieves a PENDING booking.
* App calls backend create-order endpoint.
* User approves payment with PayPal Sandbox buyer account.
* App calls backend capture-order endpoint.
* If capture succeeds, show success screen.
* If capture fails/cancels, show clear error message.

7. Sandbox test accounts
   Use PayPal Developer Sandbox accounts:

* Business sandbox account = merchant/seller
* Personal sandbox account = buyer/customer

Add README instructions explaining:

* Go to PayPal Developer Dashboard
* Open Sandbox Accounts
* Create or use a Business sandbox account
* Create or use a Personal sandbox account
* Use Business app credentials as PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID and PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET
* Use the Personal sandbox account to test paying
* Review test transactions at:
  https://www.sandbox.paypal.com/

8. Data model
   If needed, add a Payment entity/table with fields like:

* id
* bookingId
* provider = PAYPAL
* paypalOrderId
* paypalCaptureId
* amount
* currency
* status
* rawResponse or responseSummary
* createdAt
* updatedAt

Possible statuses:

* CREATED
* APPROVED
* CAPTURED
* FAILED
* CANCELLED

9. Error handling
   Handle:

* Invalid booking
* Booking not owned by user
* Invalid amount
* PayPal access token failure
* PayPal create order failure
* PayPal capture failure
* Duplicate capture attempts
* Network/API errors

10. Testing
    Add tests or manual test documentation for:

* Create PayPal order successfully
* Capture PayPal order successfully
* Booking becomes CONFIRMED only after capture
* Failed capture does not confirm booking
* Secret is not exposed to frontend
* Sandbox mode is used

Before coding:
Inspect the current backend booking/payment structure, entities, enums, auth user context, and checkout screens. Reuse existing architecture where possible. Keep the code clean, typed, and consistent with the current project style.

--- 1607. ASSISTANT ---
PayPal Sandbox integration is in place end-to-end. Here is what was added and how to use it.

## Backend (sandbox only)

- **Payment model**: `Payment` entity with `PAYPAL` provider and statuses `CREATED`, `APPROVED`, `CAPTURED`, `FAILED`, `CANCELLED`
- **PayPal service**: OAuth token, create order, capture order; amount/currency from the **booking on the server**
- **Endpoints**:
  - `POST /api/v1/payments/paypal/create-order` — body: `bookingId`, `eventId`, optional `timeSlotId`
  - `POST /api/v1/payments/paypal/capture-order` — body: `paypalOrderId`, `bookingId`
- **Booking flow**: new bookings start as `PENDING`; only move to `CONFIRMED` after successful capture
- **Security**: `PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET` stays on the server; JWT required; booking ownership validated
- **Config**: `backend/.env.example` + `application.yml` default to `https://api-m.sandbox.paypal.com`
- **Docs**: [backend/docs/PAYPAL_SANDBOX.md](backend/docs/PAYPAL_SANDBOX.md)
- **Tests**: `PayPalPaymentServiceImplTest` (3 cases) — all passing

## Mobile checkout

- **`paymentsApi.ts`**: create-order / capture-order (no secrets)
- **`payment.tsx`**: for API events → create `PENDING` booking → PayPal approval via `expo-web-browser` → capture → confirmation
- **`bookingDraftStore`**: `pendingBookingId` + `clearCheckoutSession()`
- **i18n**: PayPal Sandbox strings (EN/AR)
- **Deep links**: `vibook://paypal-return` (scheme already in `app.json`)

Non-API listings still use the simulated card flow.

## Setup

1. [PayPal Developer Dashboard](https://developer.paypal.com/dashboard/) → Sandbox app → copy Client ID & Secret  
2. [Sandbox accounts](https://developer.paypal.com/tools/sandbox/) — Business (merchant) + Personal (buyer)  
3. Backend env:

```bash
PAYPAL_MODE=sandbox
PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID=...
PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET=...
PAYPAL_BASE_URL=https://api-m.sandbox.paypal.com
```

4. Run backend with Java 21, mobile with `EXPO_PUBLIC_API_URL` pointing at your API  
5. Book an event → Pay with PayPal → log in as **Personal** sandbox buyer → review at [sandbox.paypal.com](https://www.sandbox.paypal.com/)

## Manual test checklist

| Check | Expected |
|--------|----------|
| Create booking | `PENDING` |
| Create PayPal order | `approvalUrl` returned |
| Approve in sandbox | Buyer login succeeds |
| Capture | Booking `CONFIRMED`, payment `CAPTURED` |
| Cancel PayPal | Booking stays `PENDING`, clear message |
| Failed capture | Booking not confirmed |
| Mobile env | No `PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET` |

--- 1615. ASSISTANT ---
Here is a safe setup guide. **Do not put your Client ID or Secret in `application.yml` or any committed file.**

**Security note:** You pasted live Sandbox credentials in this chat. Consider **rotating the secret** in the [PayPal Developer Dashboard](https://developer.paypal.com/dashboard/) → your Sandbox app → regenerate secret, then update only `backend/.env`.

---

## 1. Which file to edit?

| File | Use |
|------|-----|
| **`backend/.env`** | **Yes** — paste PayPal (and DB/JWT) values here locally |
| **`backend/application.yml`** | **No** — only `${PAYPAL_*}` placeholders; never real secrets |

Copy the template first:

```bash
cp backend/.env.example backend/.env
```

Edit **`backend/.env`**. Use **no spaces** around `=` (your message had spaces after `=`; that breaks loading):

```properties
PAYPAL_MODE=sandbox
PAYPAL_BASE_URL=https://api-m.sandbox.paypal.com
PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID=<paste your client id>
PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET=<paste your secret>
PAYPAL_RETURN_URL=vibook://paypal-return
PAYPAL_CANCEL_URL=vibook://paypal-cancel
```

Also set `DB_*` and `JWT_SECRET` in the same file if needed (see `.env.example`).

---

## 2. Git ignore

`backend/.env` is ignored by:

- Repo root `.gitignore` (`.env`)
- **`backend/.gitignore`** (added for clarity)

Verified: `git check-ignore backend/.env` → ignored. It will **not** be committed if you only use `.env`.

---

## 3. How the backend reads values

`application.yml` maps env → config (no hardcoded secrets):

```yaml
vibook.paypal:
  mode: ${PAYPAL_MODE:sandbox}
  client-id: ${PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID:}
  client-secret: ${PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET:}
  base-url: ${PAYPAL_BASE_URL:https://api-m.sandbox.paypal.com}
```

`spring.config.import: optional:file:.env[.properties]` was added so Spring loads **`backend/.env`** when you start from **`backend/`**.

PayPal calls enforce sandbox via `requireSandbox()` (mode must be `sandbox`, base URL must contain `sandbox`).

---

## 4. No hardcoding

Credentials belong only in **`backend/.env`** (local, gitignored). Nothing was added to source with your ID/secret.

---

## 5. Restart the backend

From the **`backend`** directory (so `.env` is found):

```bash
cd /Users/ayazidan/Downloads/Vibook-main/Vibook/backend
export JAVA_HOME=$(/usr/libexec/java_home -v 21)
./mvnw spring-boot:run
```

Stop the old process first (Ctrl+C in that terminal), then run again.

Optional check (does **not** print the secret):

```bash
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" http://localhost:8080/api/v1/auth/login \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"email":"test","password":"test"}'
```

You should get `401` or `400`, not connection refused — that means the server is up.

---

## 6. Test PayPal flow

### Option A — helper script (recommended)

After backend is running, from **`backend/`**:

```bash
chmod +x scripts/paypal-sandbox-test.sh
./scripts/paypal-sandbox-test.sh "your-user@email.com" "your-password" <event-id> 1
```

It will:

1. Log in (JWT only in memory)
2. `POST /api/v1/bookings` → PENDING
3. `POST /api/v1/payments/paypal/create-order` → prints **`approvalUrl`** (not your secret)
4. Wait for you to approve in the browser with your **Personal Sandbox buyer**
5. `POST /api/v1/payments/paypal/capture-order` → prints `paymentStatus` / `bookingStatus`

### Option B — manual curl

**a) Login**

```bash
BASE=http://localhost:8080/api/v1
TOKEN=$(curl -sf "$BASE/auth/login" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"email":"YOUR_EMAIL","password":"YOUR_PASSWORD"}' \
  | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['token'])")
```

**b) Create PENDING booking** (use a real `eventId` from your DB)

```bash
BOOKING_ID=$(curl -sf "$BASE/bookings" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"eventId":1,"guestsCount":1}' \
  | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['id'])")
```

**c) Create PayPal order**

```bash
curl -sf "$BASE/payments/paypal/create-order" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d "{\"bookingId\":$BOOKING_ID,\"eventId\":1}" | python3 -m json.tool
```

Copy **`approvalUrl`** → open in a browser → sign in with **Personal Sandbox** buyer → approve.

**d) Capture**

```bash
# Set PAYPAL_ORDER_ID from the create-order response
curl -sf "$BASE/payments/paypal/capture-order" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d "{\"paypalOrderId\":\"$PAYPAL_ORDER_ID\",\"bookingId\":$BOOKING_ID}" | python3 -m json.tool
```

Expected: `paymentStatus`: `CAPTURED`, `bookingStatus`: `CONFIRMED`.

### Mobile

Same flow in the app: checkout → Pay with PayPal → approve in browser → confirmation.

---

## Logs

Spring SQL logging is on in dev; PayPal code does **not** log `PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET`. Avoid setting `logging.level.org.springframework.web=DEBUG` for PayPal calls if you want minimal HTTP noise.

---

**Committed changes (safe):** `application.yml` (`.env` import), `backend/.gitignore`, `backend/.env.example` note, `backend/scripts/paypal-sandbox-test.sh`, doc update. **You still need to create `backend/.env` locally** with your credentials (the editor blocked auto-writing secrets, which is intentional).

--- 1618. ASSISTANT ---
# Vibook: A Bilingual Mobile and Web Platform for Event Discovery, Business Onboarding, and Bookings in Jordan

## Keywords

Event discovery; mobile application; Spring Boot; JWT authentication; business onboarding; booking management; bilingual localization (Arabic/English); admin moderation; Jordan; tourism and entertainment; React Native (Expo); MySQL

## Abstract

Jordan’s entertainment and tourism sectors rely heavily on fragmented channels—social media posts, informal messaging, and word-of-mouth—which make it difficult for residents and visitors to discover verified events, compare offerings across governorates, and complete bookings with clear status tracking. Small and medium businesses face a parallel challenge: limited digital tooling to register professionally, publish structured event listings, and manage reservations at scale. These gaps reduce transparency for consumers, increase operational overhead for organizers, and constrain the sector’s ability to present a cohesive digital presence aligned with national tourism and lifestyle goals.

**Vibook** is proposed as an integrated software solution comprising a cross-platform mobile client, a RESTful backend API, and an administrative web dashboard. The system targets the Jordanian market through governorate-based discovery (twelve governorates), category and subcategory filtering, Jordan-dinar (JOD) pricing, and full **English and Arabic** support with right-to-left layout where appropriate. Three primary roles structure access: **end users** who browse, favorite, book, and rate events; **business partners** who apply for profiles, submit listings for review, and manage events and incoming bookings after approval; and **administrators** who moderate registrations, content, bookings, ratings, and user reports via a dedicated dashboard with analytics summaries.

Core consumer functionality includes authenticated **event search and detail views**, **favorites**, a **checkout and booking flow** with statuses (*pending*, *confirmed*, *completed*, *cancelled*), and **post-booking ratings** where policy permits. Partners use a **business hub** on mobile to draft and submit profiles (*draft*, *pending review*, *approved*, *rejected*), create and maintain **business events** (including time slots and media references), and update booking status. **PayPal Sandbox** integration on the server side supports test payments for API-backed event bookings: orders are created and captured only on the backend, with secrets never exposed to clients, and bookings are confirmed only after successful capture. Administrators operate **business-profile approval workflows**, **event and booking moderation**, **rating moderation**, **report handling** (content and user-submitted issues), and **category/governorate** oversight; booking records expose payment metadata for operational visibility.

Technically, Vibook is implemented with **React Native (Expo)** and **TypeScript** for the mobile app, **Spring Boot 3** and **Java 21** for the API, **MySQL** with JPA for persistence, and **JWT-based authentication** with refresh tokens and role-based route protection. The **admin dashboard** uses **React**, **TypeScript**, and **Vite**. Security emphasizes stateless sessions, BCrypt password hashing, server-side payment orchestration, and separation of admin routes. The architecture follows a client–server model suitable for future production hardening (migrations, stricter CORS, live payment providers).

Expected benefits include easier cross-governorate discovery for users, a standardized onboarding path for local businesses, and improved trust through moderation and traceable booking states. For Jordan’s tourism and entertainment ecosystem, Vibook demonstrates how a localized, bilingual platform can centralize discovery and reservations while remaining extensible—additional verticals and production payment channels can build on the same API, subject to further implementation beyond the current event-focused, sandbox-payment scope of the graduation prototype.

--- 1622. ASSISTANT ---
# 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Project Description

**Overview**

Vibook is a bilingual digital platform for discovering and booking events in Jordan. It consists of a mobile application for consumers and business partners, a central backend service, and an administrative web dashboard. The system brings together event listings, user accounts, partner onboarding, reservations, ratings, and platform oversight in one coordinated solution.

**Purpose and significance**

The project addresses the lack of a unified, trustworthy channel for finding and booking local events across Jordan’s governorates. Many businesses still promote activities through informal or scattered channels, while users struggle to compare options, track booking status, and interact with organizers in a consistent way. Vibook supports digital transformation in the events, tourism, and entertainment sectors by offering structured discovery, a clear booking process, and moderated business participation—aligned with national interest in stronger local tourism and leisure offerings.

**Problem addressed**

In Jordan’s events and related lifestyle sectors (including entertainment, dining-oriented listings, sports, and learning-style activities represented in the platform’s category structure), consumers often face fragmented information and uncertain booking outcomes. Business organizers lack a standard path to register, publish events, and manage reservations. Administrators need tools to review new partners, oversee content, and handle reports. Vibook targets these gaps through governed partner onboarding, searchable event catalogs filtered by region and category, and traceable booking states from request to confirmation.

**Target users and stakeholders**

| Stakeholder | Role | Main needs |
|-------------|------|------------|
| **End users** | Residents and visitors who browse and book events | Reliable search, favorites, clear pricing in Jordanian dinar, booking status visibility, ratings, and Arabic/English support |
| **Business partners** | Organizers who apply to list and manage events | Simple application and approval process, event and booking management, and visibility after approval |
| **Administrators** | Platform operators | Review of business applications, moderation of events, bookings, and ratings, handling of user reports, and overview of platform activity |

Guests may browse parts of the experience without signing in; full booking and partner features require registered accounts.

**Main goals and objectives**

1. **Event discovery** — Enable users to explore events by governorate, category, and related filters.  
2. **Booking management** — Support creating and tracking bookings with defined statuses (pending, confirmed, completed, cancelled).  
3. **Business onboarding** — Allow partners to submit business profiles, pass an admin review workflow, and then publish and manage events.  
4. **Localization** — Provide English and Arabic interfaces, including right-to-left layout for Arabic, suited to the Jordanian market.  
5. **Secure access** — Protect user and admin actions through authenticated sign-in and role-appropriate access.  
6. **Admin moderation** — Give administrators tools to approve businesses, moderate content, manage bookings, and resolve reports.  
7. **User experience** — Deliver a coherent flow from discovery through checkout and confirmation on mobile, plus a dedicated admin console for operations.  
8. **Scalability** — Use a modular client–server design so additional features and partners can be added over time.

**Measuring success**

Project success can be assessed through:

- **Functional completion** — Core flows work end-to-end: discovery, booking, partner onboarding, payments in sandbox test mode, ratings, favorites, and admin moderation.  
- **Workflow accuracy** — Bookings follow the intended status rules; partner approval gates access to publishing.  
- **Security** — Sensitive operations require authentication; payment credentials remain on the server.  
- **User experience** — Interfaces are usable in both languages and support typical tasks without unnecessary steps.  
- **Operational effectiveness** — Administrators can review partners, bookings, and reports from the dashboard.  
- **Readiness for demonstration** — The system runs locally with documented setup and supports a graduation prototype suitable for academic evaluation.

| **Documentation** | Produced project guides, architecture notes, PayPal sandbox instructions, and handoff material. This preserved knowledge for reviewers, future maintainers, and the graduation report itself. |

--- 1624. ASSISTANT ---
# Scope of the Work

## Overview

The scope of this graduation project is the design and development of **Vibook**, a digital platform that supports the discovery, promotion, and booking of events in Jordan. The work covers the full operational cycle from a user finding an activity, through a business listing and managing that activity, to platform staff overseeing quality and trust. The project is intentionally bounded around **event-oriented services** within the broader tourism, entertainment, and lifestyle context: helping people find things to do, helping organizers reach an audience, and giving administrators the means to keep the marketplace orderly and credible.

The deliverable is a working prototype that demonstrates how these parties can interact through a single ecosystem rather than through disconnected channels such as informal social media posts or unstructured messaging.

## Stakeholders and Their Roles

### End Users

End users are residents and visitors who use Vibook to learn what is happening in their governorate or elsewhere in Jordan, compare options, and complete bookings when they decide to attend an event. They may browse as guests or register for a fuller experience that includes saving favorites, managing reservations, and leaving ratings after eligible bookings. Their role is to drive demand and to provide feedback that helps others choose confidently.

### Business Partners

Business partners are individuals or organizations that wish to offer events to the public—such as entertainment venues, dining-related experiences, sports activities, or learning-oriented sessions as reflected in the platform’s service categories. They apply to join the platform, submit business information for review, and—once accepted—publish events and respond to incoming bookings. Their role is to supply the catalog of bookable experiences and to maintain accurate, up-to-date offerings.

### Administrators

Administrators represent the platform operator. They review partner applications, moderate listings and user-generated content, oversee bookings and reports, and maintain reference information (such as geographic regions and event categories) that structures discovery. Their role is to enforce standards, resolve disputes or policy issues, and ensure that the marketplace remains trustworthy and usable.

## How Users Interact with the Platform

Consumers primarily interact through the **mobile application**, moving from exploration and filtering to event details, reservation, and post-booking actions such as viewing booking history or submitting ratings. Business partners use the same mobile environment in a dedicated **business area** to complete their application, manage their profile, create events, and handle bookings assigned to them. Administrators interact through a **web-based dashboard** suited to review workflows, bulk oversight, and operational tasks that require a larger screen and centralized control. Together, these channels define who does what and where, without requiring every stakeholder to use every part of the system.

## Problems Addressed and Their Importance

Stakeholders currently face several recurring difficulties that Vibook is meant to reduce:

| Stakeholder | Key problems |
|-------------|----------------|
| **End users** | Difficulty finding reliable, comparable event information across regions; uncertainty about whether a booking is confirmed; limited ability to save preferences or share structured feedback |
| **Business partners** | High effort to reach new customers digitally; lack of a standard onboarding and listing process; fragmented tools for managing reservations and reputation |
| **Administrators** | Limited visibility into who is allowed to sell on the platform; reactive handling of complaints or inappropriate content without a central moderation queue |

Addressing these problems matters because the events and tourism-related sectors in Jordan depend on trust, accessibility, and reach. When discovery and booking are fragmented, consumers hesitate to commit, businesses underinvest in digital presence, and the sector as a whole misses opportunities for growth and professionalism. A coordinated platform can improve transparency, reduce friction, and support a more organized digital marketplace aligned with wider digital transformation goals.

## Boundaries of the Project

The work is scoped to the following areas:

1. **Event discovery** — Browsing and searching events with regional and categorical organization suited to Jordan.  
2. **Booking** — Requesting and tracking reservations with clear booking states from request through completion or cancellation.  
3. **Business management** — Partner registration, approval workflow, profile maintenance, event publication, and handling of bookings directed to the partner.  
4. **Platform administration** — Oversight of partners, events, bookings, ratings, and user reports, plus operational views for platform health.

Within this scope, the project emphasizes a **demonstration-ready prototype** suitable for academic evaluation: core journeys for all three stakeholder groups are implemented end-to-end, while broader lifestyle verticals or production-grade payment and hosting concerns may remain as directions for future work rather than as commitments of this graduation deliverable. Features outside this boundary—such as unrelated e-commerce models, full national tourism planning systems, or non-event service lines without booking support—are explicitly excluded so that effort remains focused on a coherent, achievable product vision.

--- 1626. ASSISTANT ---
## Stakeholders and Users

Identifying stakeholders and primary users is a fundamental step in defining the scope of any software engineering project. Stakeholders influence requirements, accept or reject outcomes, and experience the consequences of design decisions. For Vibook, three distinct groups—end users, business partners, and administrators—each interact with the platform in different ways and hold different expectations. Documenting their profiles clarifies who the system serves, what each group needs to succeed, and how their interests align with the overall purpose of improving event discovery and booking in Jordan.

---

### Stakeholder Profile — End Users

| Field | Details |
| ----- | ------- |
| **Representative** | A registered consumer (resident or visitor) who uses Vibook to find and book events in Jordan; may also include guests who browse before creating an account. |
| **Description** | Individuals seeking leisure, entertainment, dining-related experiences, sports activities, or learning-oriented events offered through the platform. They value clear information, trustworthy listings, and straightforward reservation handling in a language they prefer (Arabic or English). |
| **Type** | Primary user; beneficiary of the consumer-facing mobile experience. |
| **Responsibilities** | Browse and filter events by region and category; review event details; create and manage bookings; save favorites; submit ratings where permitted; report concerns about content or experiences; maintain accurate account information. |
| **Success Criteria** | Can discover relevant events quickly; understands pricing and booking status; completes reservations with confidence; receives confirmation of successful bookings; can manage booking history and provide feedback; finds the interface accessible in their preferred language. |
| **Involvement** | Continuous throughout the system lifecycle as the main audience for discovery and booking; provides usage feedback and ratings that shape platform quality; participates in reporting when issues arise. |
| **Deliverables** | Completed bookings; favorites and ratings; user reports on problematic content or experiences; adoption and repeat use of the platform for event discovery. |
| **Comments / Issues** | Users may be discouraged by fragmented information elsewhere; uncertainty about booking confirmation is a common pain point. Vibook aims to reduce friction through structured listings, visible booking states, and moderated partner participation. Guest access supports exploration but full booking features require registration. |

---

### Stakeholder Profile — Business Partners

| Field | Details |
| ----- | ------- |
| **Representative** | An event organizer or business owner who applies to list events on Vibook and, once approved, manages their profile, events, and incoming bookings. |
| **Description** | Small and medium businesses or independent organizers in Jordan’s events and related lifestyle sectors who need a legitimate channel to reach customers, publish timed offerings, and handle reservations without relying solely on informal promotion. |
| **Type** | Primary user; supplier of bookable content on the platform. |
| **Responsibilities** | Submit a business profile for review; maintain accurate business and contact information; create and update event listings; respond to booking requests and update booking status as appropriate; uphold platform standards for content quality and customer communication. |
| **Success Criteria** | Application and approval process is understandable; approved partners can publish events without unnecessary delay; bookings are visible and manageable; increased reach to interested customers; positive ratings and repeat bookings indicate trust in their listings. |
| **Involvement** | Active during onboarding and ongoing event operations; engages with the platform whenever new events are planned or bookings require attention; subject to administrator review at application stage and periodic moderation thereafter. |
| **Deliverables** | Approved business profiles; published and maintained event listings; handled bookings; partner-side updates to reservation status; contribution to a diverse, regionally relevant catalog. |
| **Comments / Issues** | Partners often lack a standardized digital onboarding path and struggle with visibility in crowded informal channels. Vibook addresses this through a governed approval workflow and self-service event management, though partners must accept review criteria and platform policies before and after approval. |

---

### Stakeholder Profile — Administrators

| Field | Details |
| ----- | ------- |
| **Representative** | A platform operator or authorized staff member responsible for overseeing Vibook’s quality, compliance, and day-to-day operational health. |
| **Description** | Personnel who act on behalf of the platform owner to ensure only suitable businesses are admitted, listings remain appropriate, bookings and ratings are handled fairly, and user reports are resolved. They require oversight tools rather than consumer-style browsing alone. |
| **Type** | Internal stakeholder; governance and control authority. |
| **Responsibilities** | Review and approve or reject business applications; moderate events, bookings, and ratings; manage user and content reports; maintain categories and geographic reference data; monitor platform activity; enforce policies that protect users and partners. |
| **Success Criteria** | Pending applications and reports are processed in a timely manner; inappropriate content is identified and addressed; booking and payment-related records support operational visibility; dashboard summaries aid decision-making; the platform maintains a reputation for trust and order. |
| **Involvement** | Ongoing during operation of the system; peak involvement during onboarding queues, moderation incidents, and report resolution; participates in defining and applying platform rules that affect both users and partners. |
| **Deliverables** | Approved or rejected partner applications; moderation actions on events, ratings, and bookings; resolved or escalated reports; maintained reference data; audit of platform health through operational views. |
| **Comments / Issues** | Without centralized moderation, marketplaces risk low-quality listings and unresolved disputes. Administrators depend on clear workflows and complete information about partners, bookings, and reports. Their effectiveness directly affects user trust and partner willingness to remain on the platform. |

**Figure 1 — UML Use Case Diagram of the Vibook Platform**

The use case diagram in Figure 1 illustrates how three primary actors interact with the Vibook Platform within a single system boundary. End Users register or sign in, discover events through browsing and optional search and filtering, view event details, complete bookings, manage their reservations, save favorites, and submit ratings when eligible. Business Partners apply for a business profile, maintain approved profile information, create and edit events, manage incoming bookings, and review event-related activity. Administrators govern the marketplace by reviewing applications, approving or rejecting businesses, moderating events and ratings, overseeing bookings and reports, and monitoring overall platform activity. Include relationships represent mandatory steps—such as authentication before booking and profile approval before event management—while extend relationships capture optional or conditional behaviour, such as advanced filtering during browsing and rating submission following a completed booking. Together, these interactions define the functional scope of Vibook as a coordinated environment for event discovery, partner participation, and platform administration in Jordan.

### Corrections applied

| Change | Rationale |
|--------|-----------|
| Removed `Save Favorites <<extend>> Manage Bookings` | Favorites is a standalone consumer action. |
| Removed `Submit Ratings <<extend>> Manage Bookings` | Ratings is independent; eligibility after a confirmed booking is a business rule, not a UML extend link. |
| `Browse Events <<include>> Search & Filter Events` | Filtering is part of the discovery flow, not an optional extension of browsing. |
| Removed `Create Events <<include>> Manage Business Profile` | Profile approval is a prerequisite, not an included sub-step. |
| Renamed partner/admin booking use cases | Avoids ambiguity: **Manage Partner Bookings** and **Manage Platform Bookings**. |
| Added missing End User, Partner, and Admin use cases | Aligned with implemented Vibook features. |

**Note (for report text, not on diagram):** *Submit Ratings* is available only for bookings in a confirmed or completed state.

**Figure 1 caption paragraph**

The corrected use case diagram presents the Vibook Platform as a single system boundary serving three actor groups. End Users authenticate, discover events through browsing and integrated search and filtering, view details, book events, manage reservations and booking history, maintain profiles and payment methods, and interact with favorites and ratings as independent functions. Business Partners apply for and manage approved profiles, create and maintain events—including photos and visibility—handle incoming bookings, and review ratings and event-related activity. Administrators govern the marketplace through application review, content and booking moderation, user and reference-data management, report handling, and dashboard monitoring. Include relationships denote mandatory sub-behaviour within a use case; prerequisites such as an approved business profile before event creation are documented as business rules rather than incorrect include dependencies.

--- 1637. ASSISTANT ---
# Vibook Payments System — Technical Audit Report

**Scope:** Backend (`backend/`), Mobile (`mobile/`), Admin Web (`admin-web/`)  
**Audit basis:** Source code, configuration, tests, and documentation only (no assumptions beyond verified implementation)  
**Date:** June 2026

---

# 1. Current Implementation

## 1.1 Payment-related entities

| Item | Purpose | State |
|------|---------|-------|
| **`Payment`** (`payments` table) | Stores PayPal transaction records linked to a booking | **Functional (sandbox)** — create + capture paths persist rows |
| **`PaymentMethod`** (`payment_methods` table) | Stores saved card metadata per user (brand, last4, expiry, holder) | **Functional but isolated** — not used in checkout or PayPal flow |
| **`PaymentStatus`** enum | `CREATED`, `APPROVED`, `CAPTURED`, `FAILED`, `CANCELLED` | **Partially used** — `APPROVED` and `CANCELLED` are never set in PayPal service code |
| **`PaymentProvider`** enum | `PAYPAL` only | **Functional** — single provider |

## 1.2 DTOs

| DTO | Purpose | State |
|-----|---------|-------|
| `PayPalCreateOrderRequest` | `bookingId`, `eventId`, optional `timeSlotId` | **Functional** |
| `PayPalCreateOrderResponse` | Payment/booking IDs, PayPal order ID, `approvalUrl`, amount, statuses | **Functional** |
| `PayPalCaptureOrderRequest` | `paypalOrderId`, `bookingId` | **Functional** |
| `PayPalCaptureOrderResponse` | Payment/booking IDs, capture ID, statuses | **Functional** |
| `AdminBookingPaymentInfo` | Payment summary on admin booking responses | **Functional** |
| `AddPaymentMethodRequest` / `PaymentMethodResponse` | Saved card CRUD | **Functional** — separate from PayPal |

## 1.3 Repositories

| Repository | Methods | State |
|------------|---------|-------|
| **`PaymentRepository`** | `findByPaypalOrderId`, `findTopByBooking_IdOrderByCreatedAtDesc`, `existsByBooking_IdAndStatusIn` | **Functional** — no list/pagination APIs exposed |
| **`PaymentMethodRepository`** | User-scoped CRUD queries | **Functional** |

## 1.4 Services

| Service | Purpose | State |
|---------|---------|-------|
| **`PayPalPaymentServiceImpl`** | Create/capture orders, validate ownership, server-side amount, booking confirmation | **Functional (sandbox)** |
| **`PayPalApiClient`** | OAuth token, PayPal create/capture REST calls | **Functional (sandbox only)** |
| **`PaymentMethodServiceImpl`** | Add/list/set-default/delete saved cards (last4 only persisted) | **Functional** — disconnected from bookings |

## 1.5 Controllers & API endpoints

| Endpoint | Auth | State |
|----------|------|-------|
| `POST /api/v1/payments/paypal/create-order` | `USER`, `ADMIN` | **Functional** |
| `POST /api/v1/payments/paypal/capture-order` | `USER`, `ADMIN` | **Functional** |
| `POST/GET/PATCH/DELETE /api/v1/users/me/payment-methods` | `USER` | **Functional** — unrelated to PayPal checkout |
| `POST /api/v1/bookings` | `USER` | **Functional** — creates `PENDING` booking |

**Not implemented:** `GET /payments`, admin payment list, refund endpoints, webhook endpoints, receipt endpoints.

## 1.6 Configuration

| Item | State |
|------|-------|
| `PayPalProperties` + `application.yml` | **Functional** — env-driven credentials |
| `PayPalConfig` (`RestClient` → sandbox base URL) | **Functional** |
| `requireSandbox()` | **Enforced** on every PayPal API call — live mode blocked in code |
| `backend/.env` + `spring.config.import` | **Functional** for local secret loading |
| `backend/docs/PAYPAL_SANDBOX.md`, `scripts/paypal-sandbox-test.sh` | **Documentation / manual test tooling** |

## 1.7 Verification checklist (requested items)

| Capability | Status |
|------------|--------|
| **PayPal integration** | **Implemented** — OAuth, create order, capture |
| **Sandbox integration** | **Implemented only** — live PayPal explicitly disabled |
| **Order creation** | **Implemented** — persists `Payment` with `CREATED` |
| **Payment capture** | **Implemented** — sets `CAPTURED`, confirms booking |
| **Transaction storage** | **Partial** — `payments` table + truncated `responseSummary` (max 2000 chars) |
| **Payment history** | **Not implemented** — no user-facing history API or UI |
| **Refund support** | **Not implemented** — no code references |
| **Webhook handling** | **Not implemented** — no webhook controller or verification |
| **Booking–payment linkage** | **Implemented** — `Payment.booking` FK; admin reads latest payment per booking |

## 1.8 Tests

| Test | State |
|------|-------|
| `PayPalPaymentServiceImplTest` (4 cases) | **Implemented** — amount from server, capture confirms booking, failed capture, duplicate capture idempotency |
| Integration/E2E PayPal tests | **Not implemented** |
| Mobile/admin payment tests | **Not implemented** |

---

# 2. Database Analysis

## 2.1 Payment-related tables

### `payments`

| Column | Notes |
|--------|-------|
| `id` | PK |
| `booking_id` | FK → `bookings.id`, `nullable = false` |
| `provider` | `PAYPAL` |
| `paypal_order_id` | varchar(64), **no unique constraint** |
| `paypal_capture_id` | varchar(64), nullable until capture |
| `amount`, `currency` | Server-derived from booking/event |
| `status` | PaymentStatus enum string |
| `response_summary` | Truncated PayPal JSON |
| `created_at`, `updated_at` | Timestamps |

### `payment_methods`

| Column | Notes |
|--------|-------|
| `user_id` | FK → `users.id` |
| `brand`, `card_last4`, expiry, `card_holder_name`, `is_default` | Metadata only — full PAN/CVC not stored |

## 2.2 Relationships

```
User ──< Booking ──< Payment
         │
         └──> BusinessEvent (event_id)
User ──< PaymentMethod   (no link to Payment or Booking)
```

- **Users ↔ Payments:** indirect via `Booking`
- **Bookings ↔ Payments:** one-to-many possible; code uses latest payment per booking for admin display
- **Events ↔ Payments:** indirect via `Booking.businessEvent`
- **PaymentMethod:** standalone wallet feature; **not referenced** by `Payment` or checkout

## 2.3 Constraints & gaps

| Present | Missing / weak |
|---------|----------------|
| JPA `nullable = false` on key fields | **Unique index** on `paypal_order_id` |
| FK via `@JoinColumn` | **Explicit DB migrations** (Flyway/Liquibase) — uses `ddl-auto: update` |
| | `user_id` on `payments` for direct queries |
| | `idempotency_key` / capture attempt tracking |
| | Refund fields (`refund_id`, `refunded_at`, amount) |
| | Webhook event log table |
| | Link between `payment_methods` and `payments` |

## 2.4 Production readiness of schema

**Not production-ready.** Suitable for graduation prototype with JPA auto-update. Needs versioned migrations, unique constraints, indexes on `booking_id` / `paypal_order_id`, and audit/refund columns before launch.

---

# 3. Backend Flow Analysis

## 3.1 Implemented flow (API-backed event booking)

```
1. User authenticated (JWT)
2. POST /api/v1/bookings
   → Booking created with status PENDING
   → totalPriceJod = event.priceJod × guestsCount (no service fees)
3. POST /api/v1/payments/paypal/create-order
   → Validates: booking owned by user, PENDING, event match, no open payment (CREATED/APPROVED)
   → PayPal OAuth + POST /v2/checkout/orders
   → Payment row: CREATED, paypalOrderId, amount from booking
   → Returns approvalUrl
4. User approves in PayPal (client-side WebBrowser)
5. POST /api/v1/payments/paypal/capture-order
   → Validates ownership + payment/booking match
   → If already CAPTURED: idempotent return (no second PayPal call)
   → PayPal POST .../capture
   → If COMPLETED: Payment CAPTURED, booking CONFIRMED
   → If not COMPLETED: Payment FAILED, booking stays PENDING
```

## 3.2 Missing / incomplete steps

| Step | Gap |
|------|-----|
| PayPal cancel | User cancel does not set `Payment.CANCELLED` |
| PayPal approve-only state | `APPROVED` status never recorded before capture |
| Webhook confirmation | Capture depends entirely on mobile calling backend after browser return |
| Pending booking timeout | No expiry/cleanup of abandoned PENDING bookings |
| Cancel booking + payment | `cancelMyBooking` does not update linked `Payment` |
| Service fees | Mobile shows ~5% fees; **server charges booking total without fees** |
| Admin/business confirm without payment | Business/admin can change booking status independently of payment state |
| Retry payment | No dedicated “resume payment” endpoint for existing PENDING booking |

---

# 4. Mobile App Integration

| Screen / module | Status | Notes |
|-----------------|--------|-------|
| **`checkout.tsx`** | **Implemented** | Line items + fees display; navigates to `/payment`; fees are client-only |
| **`payment.tsx`** | **Partially implemented** | PayPal flow for `draft.apiEventId` + `vertical === 'event'` only; simulated pay for other verticals |
| **`confirmation.tsx`** | **Partially implemented** | Shows booking ID; no payment ID, capture ID, or receipt |
| **`booking/[id].tsx`** | **Partially implemented** | Maps `PENDING` → `pending_payment` but displays `totalPriceJod` as paid amount when > 0; no “Pay now” retry |
| **`(premium-sheet)/payment-methods.tsx`** | **Implemented** | Lists saved cards via API |
| **`add-payment-method.tsx`** | **Implemented** | Adds card metadata; **not used in PayPal checkout** |
| **`wallet.tsx`** | **Not implemented (payments)** | Mock/local balance; not tied to PayPal or `Payment` entity |
| **`paymentsApi.ts`** | **Implemented** | `createPayPalOrder`, `capturePayPalOrder` only |
| **`paymentMethodsApi.ts`** | **Implemented** | CRUD for saved cards |
| **PayPal (`expo-web-browser`)** | **Implemented (sandbox)** | `openAuthSessionAsync` → `vibook://paypal-return` |
| **Mock payment path** | **Implemented** | Non-API drafts skip backend booking/payment entirely |

## 4.1 Mobile gaps

- No payment history screen
- No payment status on booking list/detail from `Payment` API (booking API omits payment)
- No handling when user returns from PayPal but capture fails (orphaned CREATED payment)
- Saved payment methods and wallet are **UI-only** relative to PayPal checkout
- Checkout fees shown to user **do not match** server charge amount

# 6. Missing Features Checklist

### Core PayPal / transactions
- [ ] Live PayPal environment support (currently sandbox-only by design)
- [ ] PayPal webhooks (`CHECKOUT.ORDER.APPROVED`, `PAYMENT.CAPTURE.COMPLETED`, etc.)
- [ ] Webhook signature verification
- [ ] Set `PaymentStatus.APPROVED` when order approved (pre-capture)
- [ ] Set `PaymentStatus.CANCELLED` on user/PayPal cancel
- [ ] `GET /api/v1/payments/me` — user payment history
- [ ] `GET /api/v1/payments/me/{id}` — payment detail
- [ ] `GET /api/v1/admin/payments` — paginated transaction list
- [ ] Payment receipts / email confirmation tied to capture
- [ ] Refund API (full/partial) + PayPal refund integration
- [ ] Refund status on `Payment` entity
- [ ] Idempotency keys on create/capture requests
- [ ] Unique DB constraint on `paypal_order_id`
- [ ] Payment–booking amount reconciliation job
- [ ] Align mobile service fees with server charge (or remove fees from UI)

### Booking integration
- [ ] Resume payment for existing PENDING booking (mobile + API)
- [ ] Cancel/update payment when booking cancelled
- [ ] Block admin/business manual CONFIRM without captured payment (policy)
- [ ] Expire stale PENDING bookings and open CREATED payments
- [ ] Include payment summary in `BookingResponse` for mobile

### Mobile
- [ ] Payment retry from booking detail
- [ ] Payment status and PayPal reference on confirmation screen
- [ ] Payment history screen
- [ ] Remove or wire mock/simulated payment for production
- [ ] PayPal for non-event verticals (if in scope)
- [ ] Deep link handler for `vibook://paypal-return` / cancel routes
- [ ] Fix booking detail “paid” display for PENDING bookings

### Admin
- [ ] Dedicated Payments page (filter by status, date, user, event)
- [ ] Revenue / captured volume analytics
- [ ] Refund initiation UI
- [ ] Export transactions (CSV)
- [ ] Payment audit log (beyond truncated JSON in `response_summary`)

### Saved cards / wallet
- [ ] Decide product role: remove `PaymentMethod` or integrate with a PSP (not raw card storage)
- [ ] Remove mock wallet or connect to real ledger
- [ ] PCI-compliant card tokenization (if card payments planned)

### Infrastructure / quality
- [ ] Flyway/Liquibase migrations
- [ ] PayPal integration tests (WireMock or sandbox CI)
- [ ] E2E mobile payment test
- [ ] Production secrets management
- [ ] Rate limiting on payment endpoints
- [ ] Structured payment audit logging (no secret leakage)
- [ ] Monitoring/alerting on capture failures

### Already implemented ✓
- [x] Create PayPal orders API
- [x] Capture PayPal payments API
- [x] Store transactions (`payments` table)
- [x] Sandbox-only enforcement
- [x] Server-side amount validation
- [x] Duplicate open-payment guard on create
- [x] Duplicate capture idempotency
- [x] Booking confirmed only after successful capture
- [x] JWT protection on payment endpoints
- [x] Admin booking payment visibility (embedded)
- [x] Unit tests for core PayPal service logic
- [x] Sandbox documentation and shell test script
